editionStmt.p: The five Ethiopian manuscripts belonging to the Ferdinando Martini collection at the Forteguerriana Library in Pistoia were digitized in May–June 2018 by Antonella Brita, Karsten Helmholz, and Susanne Hummel, as part of a cooperative project for the documentation and digitization of Ethiopian manuscript collections in Italian libraries—including the non-invasive sampling of materials for scientific analysis—directed by Alessandro Bausi (University of Hamburg) and coordinated by Antonella Brita (University of Hamburg and, since 2021, the University of Florence). The project was conducted under the auspices of the Hiob Ludolf Centre for Ethiopian and Eritrean Studies (HLCEES) and the Centre for the Study of Manuscript Cultures (CSMC) at the University of Hamburg. This initiative was funded by the Centre for the Study of Manuscript Cultures (CSMC); by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) through the project (TP C05) ‘“Parchment Saints” – The Making of Ethiopian Hagiographic Manuscripts: Matter and Devotion in Manuscript Practices of Medieval and Pre-Modern Ethiopia’—part of the cooperative research unit (Sonderforschungsbereich) 950 ‘Manuskriptkulturen in Asien, Afrika und Europa’ at the University of Hamburg (2015–2019); and by the European Research Council under the European Union’s Seventh Framework Programme (FP7/2007–2013) IDEAS, Advanced Grant Agreement No. 338756, for the project ‘TraCES – From Translation to Creation: Changes in Ethiopic Style and Lexicon from Late Antiquity to the Middle Ages’ at the University of Hamburg (2014–2019).

publicationStmt.publisher: Die Schriftkultur des christlichen Äthiopiens und Eritreas: Eine multimediale Forschungsumgebung / Beta maṣāḥǝft

availability.licence: This file is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0.

list.item: digital images by Antonella Brita, Karsten Helmholz, and Susanne Hummel 2018
